subject: English Language Arts
topic: Novel Study: Long Way Down by Jason Reynolds

classroom_overview.title: Long Way Down Launch Organizer
classroom_overview.subtitle: Structure, pressure, questions, and early thinking
classroom_overview.prompt: Before or during the next reading section, track what you notice about structure, what pressure Will is facing, and what question you are carrying forward. Use page references or short evidence notes, not copied passages.
success_criteria: I noticed something about structure, repetition, or pattern.
I connected a moment to Will's pressure or conflict.
I wrote a genuine question for the next section.

reading_response.title: Long Way Down Reading Response
reading_response.subtitle: Track structure, tension, and character choice
reading_response.learning_target: I can explain how structure and voice shape my understanding of a character's conflict.
reading_response.directions: After reading the assigned section, choose one moment that changes how you understand Will. Use a page reference or brief evidence note. Do not copy a long passage.
reading_response.prompt: Focus question: How does the elevator structure affect the way we understand Will's thoughts, memories, or choices?

vocabulary.definition: a novel told through poems or poetic sections
a struggle happening inside a character
how a text is organized and how that organization shapes meaning

success_criteria: I named a specific moment or section.
I used a page reference or brief evidence note.
I explained how the structure affects meaning.
I connected the moment to Will's conflict or choice.

theme_cer.title: Long Way Down CER Organizer
theme_cer.subtitle: Claim, evidence, reasoning about theme
theme_cer.question: What is the novel suggesting about rules, grief, revenge, or choice?
theme_cer.learning_target: I can develop a theme claim and support it with evidence from the novel.
theme_cer.directions: Choose one theme idea. Make a claim about what the novel is suggesting. Use page references, short evidence notes, or teacher-approved brief quotations. Explain how the evidence supports your claim.
sentence_starters: One moment that supports this is when...
The reader is invited to question...
success_criteria: My claim is about a theme, not just a plot event.
My evidence points to a specific moment in the novel.
My reasoning explains how the evidence supports the theme.
I avoided copying long passages from the book.

floor_encounter_organizer.title: Elevator Encounter Tracker
floor_encounter_organizer.subtitle: How each encounter changes the pressure on Will
floor_encounter_organizer.prompt: Track the assigned elevator-floor encounter. Focus on what the encounter reveals, what it makes Will remember, and how it affects his decision-making.
success_criteria: I identified what the encounter reveals about the past.
I explained how the encounter changes tension or uncertainty.
I connected the encounter to Will's choice.

exit_reflection.title: Long Way Down Exit Reflection
exit_reflection.subtitle: Question, tension, and next reading move
exit_reflection.learning_target: I can reflect on how my thinking changed during today's reading.
exit_reflection.prompt: What question are you carrying into the next section? What detail or structural choice created that question?
success_criteria: I named the detail or structure that created the question.
I explained why the question matters to my understanding of Will.
